title: La Fortuna, Costa Rica: The Complete Travel and Photography Guide for 2026
excerpt: La Fortuna sits at the foot of Arenal Volcano and has evolved from a quiet farming town into one of Costa Rica's most complete travel destinations. This guide covers the town's character, the surrounding attractions, and how to photograph the area in ways that go beyond the standard volcano postcard.
content:
La Fortuna, Costa Rica: The Complete Travel and Photography Guide for 2026

La Fortuna grew up in the shadow of Arenal, and the relationship between town and volcano is the defining fact of both. The 1968 eruption buried the western flank of the mountain under ash and changed the trajectory of the region's development — forcing the abandonment of the old town of Arenal and creating the conditions that eventually made La Fortuna into the tourism hub it is today. Walking through the town, you are always aware of the volcano somewhere behind you, even when the summit is cloud-shrouded.

The Town and Its Character

La Fortuna retains a more genuinely Costa Rican character than most of the country's tourist towns. The main street runs parallel to the volcano, and on any given evening the central park fills with families, teenagers on motorcycles, and older men playing chess at the outdoor tables. The tourism infrastructure is concentrated along the main road — restaurants, tour operators, gear shops — but the town itself does not feel like a theme park version of Costa Rica.

The town's location at 250 meters elevation means it is noticeably warmer than the highlands to the south, but the volcano's influence creates afternoon cloud build-up that frequently produces rain showers in the afternoon. The best weather window tends to be from December through April, when morning skies are clearer and the volcano is more visible.

Photography Around Arenal Volcano

Arenal is one of the most satisfying volcano subjects I have worked with. The cone is steep and symmetrical, which creates a strong geometric element in landscape compositions, and the summit cloud behavior varies enough from day to day that no two visits produce the same image. The volcano is best photographed in the early morning, when convective cloud cover has not yet built over the peak and the first light catches the western face in clean side light.

The classic viewpoint is from the shores of Lake Arenal, where the water provides a reflective foreground that the bare slopes above town do not. The Arenal Observatory Lodge area offers elevated angles that include the full cone, the lake, and the surrounding pastoral landscape. For aerial work, small-plane and helicopter departures are available from the La Fortuna area.

The La Fortuna Waterfall

The La Fortuna Waterfall is one of the most accessible major waterfalls in Costa Rica, requiring a 500-step descent rather than a long hike. The 70-meter cascade drops into a circular pool surrounded by forest. The water volume varies dramatically — in October it thunders; in March it runs as a thin ribbon. Both versions are worth photographing, just for different reasons.

A wide-angle lens at the pool's edge captures the full height of the falls and the surrounding vegetation. Longer exposures during the daytime produce the silky-water effect that is the hallmark of waterfall photography. A telephoto from the far side of the pool isolates the cascade against the surrounding green — a more abstract composition than the wide-angle approach.

Hot Springs — Volcanic Geology as Travel Experience

The Tabacón hot springs are the most developed option in the La Fortuna area, built around a series of natural hot springs fed by geothermal activity from the volcano. The water emerges from the ground at over 50 degrees Celsius and cools to bath temperature as it flows through the landscaped pools. The combination of warm water, tropical vegetation, and the volcano visible above the tree line makes this one of the more photogenic hot spring setups in Central America.

The Eco Termales hot springs offer a smaller, quieter alternative that some photographers prefer for its relative lack of crowds. Both locations are best photographed early in the morning before the day's visitors arrive, or in the evening when artificial lighting supplements the natural light.

Baldi and Other Hot Spring Options

Baldi Hot Springs, between Tabacón and La Fortuna town, occupies a middle ground in terms of scale and atmosphere. The property has multiple pools at different temperatures and a swim-up bar that is as much social space as bathing facility. From a photography standpoint, Baldi's advantage is that it has been designed with a more intentional aesthetic — stone-edged pools, deliberate landscaping — that produces cleaner compositions than the more natural arrangements at other springs.

Caño Negro — Wetland Photography Near La Fortuna

A day trip to Caño Negro from La Fortuna takes you into one of the most productive wetland birding and wildlife areas in Costa Rica. The flooded forest and open water channels attract jabiru storks, roseate spoonbills, wood storks, and an exceptional variety of herons. The experience is entirely water-based — moving by boat through narrow channels — which presents a different set of photography challenges and opportunities compared to the terrestrial wildlife around La Fortuna itself.

The dry season from December through April concentrates wildlife around the remaining water, making this the prime period for Caño Negro. During the green season, the area floods more extensively and some channels become impassable, but the birding remains productive.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many days do I need for La Fortuna?

Two full days covers the essential experiences — Arenal Volcano views, the waterfall, and either the hot springs or Caño Negro — without feeling rushed. A third day allows for a combination of the hots springs, a second volcano viewpoint session, and exploring the area's other trails. Four or more days begins to repeat experiences without adding significantly new dimensions.

Is La Fortuna a good base for photography?

Yes, particularly for landscape and nature work. The town is well-located for Arenal Volcano photography (both sunrise and sunset shoots are accessible), the waterfall, and the hot springs. For wildlife photography, La Fortuna is better as a base for day trips to Caño Negro, Venado caves, or the Arenal Volcano park trails rather than a destination in its own right.

What is the best time to photograph Arenal Volcano?

Early morning, between 6:00 and 8:00 AM, is the most reliable window for a clear summit. Afternoon convective clouds build over the peak by mid-morning and tend to persist through the afternoon, particularly during the green season. The dry season months of January through March offer the most consistently clear mornings.

Can I swim at the La Fortuna Waterfall?

Yes, swimming is permitted in the lower pool. The water is cold — genuinely cold, even on hot days — and the pool depth near the base of the falls is significant. Do not swim close to the base of the cascade itself. The rocks around the pool are slippery; water shoes are useful.

What should I photograph in La Fortuna besides the volcano?

The hot spring steam rising from the forest in early morning is a subject that many photographers overlook. The agricultural landscape between La Fortuna and the lake — cattle pastures, sugar cane fields, the occasional palm oil plantation — offers a human landscape dimension that the more celebrated natural attractions do not. The night sky from the elevated areas around town, when clear, shows the southern Milky Way over the volcano silhouette.
